prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 52-year-old Black man.
My blood pressure spikes when I forget my pills--I just took them now so it should come back down.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I checked my blood pressure because I've had a mild headache today and it was very high. I don't have chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness, numbness, or trouble speaking.
- History: I haven't been on blood pressure meds before.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): A very high blood pressure reading with a headache can be a warning sign. Even without chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness/numbness, or trouble speaking, severely elevated blood pressure can sometimes cause serious problems and needs prompt assessment. Because you report the reading was “very high” and you have symptoms (headache) and you’re not established on blood pressure medication, it’s safest to be evaluated urgently to confirm the blood pressure, check for organ effects (heart, brain, kidneys, eyes), and start/adjust treatment. If any new symptoms develop (chest pain, shortness of breath, confusion, weakness/numbness, trouble speaking, vision changes), that increases urgency further.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 62%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): A very high blood pressure reading along with a headache can be a warning sign. Even without chest pain, shortness of breath, vision changes, weakness/numbness, or speech trouble, severely elevated blood pressure can still put you at risk for stroke, heart problems, or kidney injury. You mention “spikes when I forget my pills,” but also that you “haven’t been on blood pressure meds before,” which is conflicting; either way, a very high reading plus symptoms should be treated as urgent until proven otherwise. Taking medication may lower it, but it’s important to be checked promptly to confirm the level, look for organ stress, and decide on safe treatment.

TRIAGE: D

CONFIDENCE: 62%

You mention “spikes when I forget my pills,” but also that you “haven’t been on blood pressure meds before,” which is conflicting—either way, a very high reading plus symptoms shouldn’t be managed only at home. Taking medication may lower it, but you still need a same-day check to confirm the level, look for complications (kidney, heart, brain), and set a safe treatment plan.

TRIAGE: D

The fact that you say your pressure “spikes when you forget my pills” suggests you are on blood pressure medicine, but you also say you haven’t been on meds before—either way, a severe high reading plus symptoms warrants urgent evaluation, especially at age 52. Taking your pills may lower it, but you shouldn’t wait at home to see if it improves.
